content.problemStatement:
Bob has an array consisting of **N** integers. He wants to perform a sequence of operations after which all the numbers in the array will be equal.

Each operation consists of choosing any **prime** integer **P**. Bob then chooses any **P** numbers from the array and replaces each of them by the **arithmetic mean** of these **P** numbers (leaving them at the same places in the array as before this operation). Note that the resulting numbers may not be integer.

Help Bob, find a sequence of operations which achieves his goal. It is guaranteed that this goal is always achievable. The sequence is **not** required to be the shortest, but it should consist of **no more than 1000** operations.
constraints: The total number of operations for a test case must not exceed 1000.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 30). The second line contains N integers A_i (1 ≤ A_i ≤ 1000) separated by single spaces.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with an integer K (0 ≤ K ≤ 1000), the number of operations. Then output K lines, each describing one operation. The i‑th operation line starts with a prime integer P_i (2 ≤ P_i ≤ N) followed by P_i distinct 1‑based indices of the chosen elements.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The initial array is (5, 6, 7, 8). The array after the first operation is (5, 6.5, 6.5, 8). The array after the second operation is (6.5, 6.5, 6.5, 6.5).
The initial array is (9, 5, 4, 9, 4, 2, 11). The array after the operation is (44/7, 44/7, 44/7, 44/7, 44/7, 44/7, 44/7).
The initial array is (42, 42, 42, 42, 42, 42). The array is already equal.

solution.approach:
The goal is to make all numbers equal using operations that replace a chosen prime‑sized subset by its arithmetic mean. The construction works recursively:
1. If the current set size S is prime, a single operation on all its indices makes every element equal to the overall mean of that set.
2. If S is composite, let p be the smallest prime divisor of S. Split the indices into groups of size p. For each group output one operation – after it all elements inside the group become equal to the group's mean.
3. After step 2 each group is uniform, so we can represent the whole set by the first index of each group. Recursively equalize this smaller set of representatives.
Because each recursion reduces the size at least by a factor of the smallest prime divisor, the total number of operations is bounded by N (far below 1000). An additional early‑exit checks whether the whole array is already uniform, in which case K = 0.
The algorithm is deterministic, runs in O(N log N) time per test case and uses O(N) extra space for storing the operations.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (the recursion depth is at most log₂ N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the list of operations.
